tasks.prompt:
The file `/app/plate.step` contains a rectangular plate (60 × 60 × 10 mm)
with a Ø10 mm through-hole centered at the origin, axis along Z.

Add two fasteners that mate through this hole:

1. **A hex socket-head cap screw conforming to DIN EN ISO 4762, size M8 × 25.**
   The head must bear against the +Z (top) face of the plate, with the
   threaded shank passing down through the plate hole.

2. **A hex nut conforming to DIN EN ISO 4032, size M8.** The nut must sit
   on the −Z (bottom) face of the plate, coaxial with the bolt, and the
   bolt's shank must extend completely through the nut to provide full
   thread engagement.

Output the complete assembly as a STEP file at `/app/out.step` containing
all three solids (plate, bolt, nut) as **distinct bodies** within a single
file. Also write an STL mesh of the assembly to `/app/out.stl`.

Notes:
  - The plate hole (Ø10) is intentionally larger than the M8 thread for
    clearance — do not resize the bolt to match the hole.
  - You must look up the standards (ISO 4762 for the cap screw, ISO 4032
    for the hex nut) to determine head dimensions, socket dimensions, nut
    thickness, and across-flats width. These are not given in this prompt.
  - For the threaded portion of the bolt and the nut bore, a plain
    cylindrical representation at the nominal thread diameter is acceptable
    — actual helical thread geometry is not required.

tasks.discriminator: Reading PDF schematics + 3D construction. Per [[findings]], frontier models spend ~25% of turn budget reading PDFs. Weaker models often misinterpret views or miss features entirely.
tasks.prompt:
`/app/Steel Plain Bore Sprocket.pdf` contains an engineering schematic. Read it and build a 3D solid model that matches the design.

Save the model as a STEP file at `/app/out.step`. The model must be a single closed manifold solid.

`/app/schematic.pdf` contains an engineering drawing of a corner bracket with multiple views (isometric, side, top, front) and all dimensions.

Build the bracket as specified in the drawing. Output:

- `/app/out.step` — single STEP body
- `/app/out.stl` — STL mesh

Match all features shown: the trapezoidal taper, all holes on every face, and all edge fillets. Pay attention to how rounded edges interact with the slanted end faces.

**Important:** Save `/app/out.step` and `/app/out.stl` as soon as you have any valid geometry, then iterate to add missing features. This way, partial progress is preserved even if later refinements fail or time runs short.

tasks.discriminator: Both chamfer and fillet operations required; agents tend to apply one not both.
tasks.prompt:
`/app/cube-chamfer-and-fillet.step` contains a 50 × 50 × 50 mm solid cube.

Modify the cube so that:
- The four top edges (highest-Z face) are chamfered at **5 mm × 45°** (equal-distance chamfer).
- The four bottom edges (lowest-Z face) are filleted at **R = 3 mm**.

The four vertical edges must remain unmodified.

Save the modified part to `/app/out.step`.

tasks.discriminator: No native CAD primitive for spiral bevel gears in any common Python CAD library. Agent must construct the gear from first principles: conical pitch surface + spiral tooth traces + involute cross-sections + boolean subtraction. Polyline-vs-BSPLINE divergence (from prior findings) should appear strongly here. Weaker models likely produce a straight bevel, polyline approximation, or fail to produce any teeth at all.
tasks.prompt:
`/app/cylinder-primitive.step` contains a solid stock cylinder, Ø 540 mm × 145 mm tall along the Z axis. The cylinder's axis is at (X = 291, Y = 0); its base sits at Z = 0 and its top at Z = 145.

Machine this stock into a spiral bevel gear with the following design:

- **Tooth count:** 64
- **Inside (toe) pitch diameter:** 325 mm
- **Outside (heel) pitch diameter:** 535 mm
- **Face width** (along pitch cone): 120 mm
- **Face cone angle** (half-angle of the outer face cone from the gear axis): 70°
- **Spiral angle at mean radius:** 35°
- **Hand:** right
- **Drill a center shaft bore through the part:** Ø 97 mm hole coaxial with the gear axis, full depth (from Z = 0 to Z = 145). The input stock has no bore — the agent must add it.
- **Gear axis:** coaxial with the stock cylinder

True involute spiral tooth flanks (B-spline surfaces). Do not approximate the tooth profile with polylines.

The gear must fit entirely within the stock cylinder envelope — no material may extend outside R = 270 from the axis or outside Z ∈ [0, 145].

Save the modified part to `/app/out.step`.

tasks.discriminator: Agent must correctly construct 4 symmetric arc slots (not generic pockets) with the right centerline radius, angular sweep, and semicircular end caps, plus a coaxial center bore. Tests catch wrong slot count, asymmetric placement, sharp corners, and blind vs through.
tasks.prompt:
`/app/flywheel-base.step` contains a 140 mm diameter steel flywheel disc, 5 mm thick, with a 40 mm raised hub at the center. The rotation axis is Z, with the hub on the +Z side (hub top at Z = 0, disc bottom at Z = 10).

Modify the part to reduce its rotational inertia:

- Round the inner corner between the hub and the disc with a **2 mm fillet**.
- Cut a **Ø 30 mm bore** along the rotation axis, all the way through.
- Cut **four arc-slot lightening windows** through the disc — one per quadrant, centered on the 45°, 135°, 225°, and 315° diagonal bisectors. Each slot spans **30°** of arc with its centerline at **R = 45 mm**; the slot is **20 mm wide** (inner arc R = 35, outer arc R = 55) and capped with semicircular ends of **R = 10 mm**.

Preserve the outer rim (R = 70), the hub diameter where it isn't bored or filleted, the hub top face at Z = 0, and the overall bounding box.

Save the modified part to `/app/out.step`.

Build a meshing pair of involute spur gears as a STEP assembly with two distinct bodies, plus an STL mesh.

Gear 1: 20 teeth, module 2, pressure angle 20°, 10 mm face width, Ø10 hub bore, centered at the origin, axis along Z.

Gear 2: 30 teeth, same specs as Gear 1 otherwise, centered at (50, 0, 0), axis along Z.

The gears must mesh — Gear 2 must be rotated about its own axis so its teeth engage Gear 1's tooth gaps at the contact point.

Write to `/app/out.step` (two distinct solids) and `/app/out.stl`.

tasks.discriminator: Three distinct features at three different angular positions on a hex prism: through-hole pair, single through-hole, blind bore. Tests hex face geometry math (across-flats vs across-corners), perpendicular drilling along face normals, and the through vs blind distinction.
tasks.prompt:
`/app/hex-prism-primitive.step` contains a solid hexagonal prism centered on the Z axis: 60 mm circumscribed diameter (30 mm side length, 51.96 mm across flats), 50 mm tall (Z ∈ [−25, +25]). The prism is "flat-top" oriented — one of the six rectangular side faces is perpendicular to the +X axis. Each side face is a 30 mm × 50 mm rectangle.

Modify the prism by drilling three sets of holes on three different side faces:

- **Pick one side face.** Drill **two Ø 10 mm holes through it, exiting on the opposite face**. The two hole centers sit on the vertical centerline of the chosen face, offset **±15 mm above and below the face's vertical mid-point** (so the holes are vertically separated by 30 mm, both at horizontal face-center).

- **On a different side face**, drill a **single Ø 20 mm hole through the face, exiting on the opposite face**. The hole is centered exactly at the chosen face's geometric center.

- **On a third side face** (one not yet drilled), make a **Ø 10 mm blind bore, 10 mm deep**, centered at the chosen face's geometric center. This bore stops inside the prism — it does not exit on the opposite face.

The three remaining side faces, the top face (Z = +25), and the bottom face (Z = −25) must be left untouched. The prism's outer hex envelope must be preserved.

Save the modified part to `/app/out.step`.
